speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11, 1996. My name is Joseph Huttler, and I'm interviewing Eva Slomovits, whose maiden name is Lax, here in Brooklyn, New York, in the United States of America. This interview will be conducted in English. Today is December 11, 1996. I'm Joseph Huttler and I'm interviewing Eva Lax Slomovits here in Brooklyn, New York, in the United States of America. This interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Eva Slomovits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva, E-V-A. Slomovits, S-L-O-M-O-V-I-T-S. My maiden name is Lax, L-A-X.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 68 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born September the 6th, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a small town in Czechoslovakia, Zarcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-A-R-C-H-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were six children and my parents, father and mother. We were four girls. Actually, we were five girls but the oldest one died before I'm-- I was born. She was seven years old. And we were four girls, and after I was the youngest girl. And after me there were two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Yitchak, and my mother's name was Miriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in Zarcha and my father came from [NON-ENGLISH], next town, small-- also small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the names of your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister, the one that died, her name was Miri. And the next one was Cilia, the next one was Lilly, Ilona, me, Eva, and Michael and Eliot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael and Eliot were your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that Miri died young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was seven years old. She died on whooping cough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something about you4-- Zarcha. What kind of town was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zarcha was a small town. There was only about 600 families, and we were over 35 Jewish families. And most of the Jewish people had businesses and not the Jewish people had fields. And it was like farms. They had cows and horses. And we had a beautiful shul. We lived near a beautiful lake. And we had a small school, Ukrainian. One school was Czech. And most of the Jewish children really went to Czech school. But by the time it was time for me to go to school, in 1938, already the Czechs weren't there. Hungarian occupied, so I went to the Ukrainian school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you grow up speaking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the house we spoke Yiddish. But-- and the-- and the peasants spoke Ukrainian. So this was our second language.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of education did you receive before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I went five years to elementary school. That was the end of my education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any religious instruction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only from my father. My father taught us how to read and to have-- pray, and history, because my father was a Hebrew teacher. So whenever he had a chance, he would teach us. The boys went to-- had Hebrew education. There was a cheder. But girls didn't get-- most of the time girls would only-- they were taking a rabbi to the house to teach them. But being-- we couldn't afford it already those years, so my father taught us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that he was a teacher. Was that his primary profession, or did he-- did he have another job?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a teacher before he got married, even. And then when he got married, he-- he was-- used to travel to Germany, would bring crystals from Czechoslovakia to-- into Germany. And he would go away like for a couple of weeks and then return again. But when my mother died, when my mother got sick already, he stopped traveling to Germany. And maybe the war was already, he couldn't. So he took up teaching again, in next town. He became a Hebrew teacher. And that was until we came to 1944, till we left there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father ever in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was in First World War. He was a soldier. And then he was captured by the Russians, and he was, matter of fact, about two years after the war, still in captivity. He didn't know that the war was over. And when he came back home, they were just shocked to see that my father came back home. They thought he was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever describe his experiences in the war to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He always talked about the war, and it was very frightening for me to know, to hear about a war, because if they would talk about a war, I always pictured what my father would say what a war is like. But it was different for us-- the war was different than for my father. He was a soldier and we were civilians, so it was entirely different for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think we were very orthodox. We had a shul, even 35 families, but there was no problem with having 10 people to daven every morning and every night. And we had a cheder-- where my brothers would go. And we had a mikveh-- a bath, ritual bath. And we had-- I mean, a shochet that he would slaughter for meat. And we were very observant, very observant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the shul have a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Ou shul didn't have a rabbi. I think that all the people practically were rabbis, had [NON-ENGLISH], and they were capable to-- we just, the community had organized a president and-- and the shul was ran by the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father-- was he ordained?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was ordained as a rabbi, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever describe to you what it was like to be orthodox and-- and be a soldier? Any--

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: During the World War I. He says he never ate treif during the war. He lived on bread and tea and rice. And through the whole time, he never ate treif-- which I can't say it for myself. Can you describe for me your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think my father was-- to me, he was a father and a mother. He-- when he came home, he would spend all the time with the children, talking to us, and explain us thing, and to teach us. And he taught us how to cook, how to bake, how whatever I learned as a child, I learned from my father. He would get up early in the morning and prepare for us things that we should have for the day, and he would come home at night to his [NON-ENGLISH]. And if he came home, his-- that time belonged to the children. He would always go with us swimming. And he taught us how to swim, which he always felt is a very important thing. And because we lived near a lake, so I don't even remember how young I was when I start swimming. Because he felt always it's important, because we lived near a lake, we should know how to swim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- I loved my father very dear, very much. What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, he was a very handsome man. He was tall, he was-- walked very straight. He was religious, but yet he understood the modern world. He would make us girls go to the movies, buy us ticket. I don't think he ever went to a movie, but he would buy tickets. He used to bring us books to read from the city, because he wanted the girls should be more modern. We should know more about the world than just our age. He says, Zarcha doesn't start and end here. The world is big, and people live different kind of lives. And I want you should know more about the world. And he used to tell us always about his trips to Germany, and he used to describe us places and things. And that's where I loved my father so much, because he was so giving of himself to the family. He always tried to talk to us about different things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: From my mother, I don't know much because I was very young when my mother died. When my-- when I was only two weeks old, my mother got sick. She had TB. And the doctor said, if she's going to nurse me, then she-- we both wouldn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father decided to send her away to a sanitarium where she would try to-- they should take care on her. So my grandma took me. And my grandma fed me. She chewed the food and gave it to me, and with a spoon she fed me milk. And I stayed with my grandma.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother got cured from the TB, but somehow I became my grandma's pet. And she wouldn't-- she lived in the same house, just a different room. But I was my grandma's pet, so I remember my grandma more. And then when I was six years old, my grandma got-- my mother had cancer, breast cancer. And she-- she went to the doctor. And the doctor told her-- it was before the holidays-- that she should wait for the holidays, she should go for surgery. And she had-- she went to a big city where they recommended a very good doctor, and she had a mastectomy. And the doctor said that she would be fine. But she-- we didn't have any insurance. My father would pay the hospitals, it was very costly. And my father-- my mother came home, and we were so happy that she's OK. But the cancer reoccurred again, and she had to go back to the hospital. She had another operation, and she came home. And then she had to go back for treatments that give her-- Those years, they already had some kind of a treatments for breast cancer. And she didn't make it. We were practically wiped out from that sickness, that my mother, because my father would sell field, my father would sell a cow to pay for the hospitals and the doctors. And she died in 1936, she-- she died. And she left six children and my father, with the six children. My sister, my older sister was only 12 years old, and my little brother was two years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know much about my mother. I remember my mother only going to the hospital, coming home from the hospital. And I never remember she should do some things for me, or she should embrace me or she should do something for me, my mother. Because I stayed with my grandma.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my grandma died a year before my mother. But when I came with my sisters, already, my mother was in and out of the hospital. So there is very little that I remember my mother. But they said-- everybody said that I look like my mother. And I think my father-- I was the fifth girl. I think he would throw me out if I wouldn't look like my mother, but he loved my mother so much. But he loved all the children. He was very kind. So my father always says, you look like your mother. My other sister look like him, and I look like my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever remarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father never remarried. He went-- he says, I don't want to bring a stepmother for my children. Says that if they-- God, HaShem didn't want they should have a mother, I'll take care of my children. And he would never even think about getting married. And sometimes we would tell him that if he would get married, our lives would be easier. He says, no. It would be harder.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a grandmother that took care of you. Was this your father's mother or your mother's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Ruchl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember very, very nice things about my grandma. I remember I slept with her all the time. And I remember when I used to come-- the weather was cold and I used to come, how she embraced me, how warm she was. And she used to take me to the oven and warm me up and-- and make me special food. And I think the children were always jealous that my-- my grandma-- she had six daughters, and she had 36 grandchildren, and she would take all the grandchildren to the lake to swimming. And they had a grocery, so she would give us always candy. So every-- she would come out with a tin of candy, and everybody was able to-- how much they were able to take candy. Well, he says you, Chavalah-- she called me Chavalah-- by the way-- you, Chavalah, have very small hands, so you could go with both hands. But there were smaller kids than me. Now, I think that because she favored me, she would let me take both-- with both hands candy. And the children always knew that I'm grandma's favorite.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember my grandma. She went someplace, she took me, too. She would go other-- to different cities, because her daughters-- two-- two daughters, three daughters lived in the same town. But the other daughters lived far away, so she would go. She would take me always with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She would never leave me. I would go-- wherever she went, I always went with her. And I remember the trips. Even I was so young, I remember the trips with my grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to [NON-ENGLISH], to Irsava, because she had there children. She had a daughter in Irsava, and she had a daughter in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were wealthier people. So, I remember the beautiful home she had, my aunt, and-- and the good food that my grandma always made me there-- special food for me. And she-- I was, like I said, the favorite for my grandma. So I remember her better than I remember my mother. Did the impact of-- Was the impact of her death greater on you than that of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I think it wasn't so bad, because I came-- with-- I stayed with my sisters, and I became like part of the family again. Because-- I-- and I want to-- I want to belong with my family, with my sisters. But that gave me a chance to come back home, like be with my sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when my mother died, I was-- I was devastated. It was very, very difficult for us-- for us children. Very hard. I took her death like a punishment from God-- that my grandma died, my mother died. Why am I being punished? I pray every morning-- I'm a good girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was so hurt. And I felt that God did it, because they said God does it. I was very angry at God that he did this to me, and at such a young age. And if I saw mothers would walk with their children, I felt very, very uncomfortable. The school, I remember something very clear. The river, after Passover, the river-- the snow would melt, the river would go over. So the teacher said that everybody should go home, because the river-- the water's getting higher. So the mothers came running to the school for their-- for their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was standing there on the steps, and I was waiting. And then I thought to myself, what am I waiting for? My mother is not going to come. And I walked home, hysterical crying, for my mother. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard times. It was-- it was very difficult for us to grow up without a mother. Also, we had a lot of responsibility as very young children because we had a farm, we had cows, and chicken. and-- we had responsibilities to take care, and do the cooking. We had help, but we still had a lot of responsibilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of help did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had not Jewish help. We had somebody would come in to do the laundry. Didn't have a washing machine-- we had to do it by hand. We were children, we couldn't do it. To clean up once a week the house. On Saturday, we had the-- the same girl would come and make fire, and milk the cows, and bring water from the well. And she would-- she did for us-- she helped-- her mother used to work for-- for my grandma. And she worked for us. So they spoke Yiddish with us. They knew how to speak Yiddish, and they even knew how to pray. She says, come on Chavalah, you get up, wash yourself, already, and start praying so I can give you something to eat. And she says, say it out loud. She want to see if I'm saying it the right way. So we were very close with the peasants there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever--did your father ever talk to you about your mother's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: About my mother's-- not really. He-- he tried to comfort us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometimes I would say, ask questions my father because he was such a religious man-- how could he do that to us? And he says, there are certain questions, certain things that we don't ask questions of God. We have to accept, because this is how God wanted it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as a young child, even till this day, it's very hard to accept that God wanted somebody-- wants somebody to suffer. But he-- he was a religious man, and he accepted it, that God wanted it this-- this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was religious life like in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Zarcha?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nice. I liked very much it. I liked came Friday-- we made a special meal always. And we-- my father was a very good cook. He cooked-- he would come home from-- earlier to cook, to help us to make Shabbos. And he would cook for us. And Saturday morning, he would go to-- Friday night he would go to temple with my brothers. The girls didn't go. And Saturday morning he would go. We sometimes go with them, and sometimes we didn't go. In the summertime we would go. And Saturday afternoon we would go for a walk there at the lake, and we would meet-- the Jewish kids would meet. We had like a meeting place where we would meet, always-- boys and girls. And we used to sing and-- and take off our shoes and go into the water. And we used to have a lot of fun, growing up near in that town. I thought it was a very pretty town, this town, this Zarcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is the sixth day of Hanukkah. Do you remember anything about how Hanukkah was celebrated or other holidays in Zarcha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A matter of fact, my father, he was not only educator, but he was very handy with his hands. And he made for-- for everybody-- for my aunts and for us-- he made like special menorah. I'm going to show it, because I-- when I went to Budapest, I found it. And he said he copied it from-- from--he was in a museum in Budapest and he saw it and he copied it. And he made like little chairs from tin, and we layered it with candles. The fifth Hanukkah candle, when he lay it, it was his birthday. So that night, we made a special meal for my father, and we all wished him and kissed him and we wished him Happy Hanukkah. And it was very festive, Hanukkah. We used to sing Hanukkah songs, and play dreidel, and play cards. And-- it was very, very-- we always looked forward to a holiday, because we made very-- a big thing out of a holiday. We didn't have much entertainment, but we made our own entertainment between the-- and the family. We would invite some cousins. We didn't buy gifts-- we didn't buy gifts for Hanukkah. But we made a special meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything-- any particular memories of the other holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other holidays, like Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur? Yes. Let's say on Yom Kipper, it was a very scary thing for me always, because the men would put on the kittel, and the women would also wear white.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they used to say that, as a child, they used to say, why white? This is-- this is when they dress people when they die, when they get married, in white. And I always thought that maybe they're going to die on Yom Kipper, for some reason, the children-- as a child. So it was scary thing for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But other holidays, Simchat Torah, I loved it. We made our own flags, and with an apple, and we would-- my father was very creative, so he made beautiful colored flags for us. And the president of the shul would always give a Kaddish, and they would bake special cookies. And they were so delicious. I remember those-- those cookies till this day. And it was very, very nice-- every holiday had a special meaning. And before Pesach, we baked our own matzos. And I remember there, near the shul, that we baked the matzohs. They had there an oven. And I remember, I was only 12 years old, I went already to roll the matzohs. And they says, no, she can't do it. She's a child. She-- she can't do it. My father put a little box for me, because I couldn't reach a table. He says, my Chavalah could do it. And they watched the matzoh till it came out from the oven-- because if it was thinner or heavier, it would burn. So my father was so proud of me, that I could do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to go to bake-- we got paid for it, and we had to pay for the-- for the-- that they bake. Let's say, we had 25 kilo flour, so we paid, let's say, two pengo a kilo. So they paid me 10 pengos a day, so, you know, it evened out. So my sisters and my brothers were worked already, and my father. So it gave us a little work, because there was very little work there. We had to make a living, so before Pesach we used to bake our own matzoh. We're going to stop here to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
